plan.description:
MultiGIS4Rivers data management plan (DMP) as developed in this document fulfills project requirements for the management of research data since the early stages (M1-M3) and after the duration of the project following the approach “as open as possible, as closed as necessary”. Guidelines as well as responsibilities and tools are set out with the aim to provide for an effective framework to support lifecycle research data management with a particular emphasis in the assessment of FAIR and Open Science principles mainly oriented to those datasets relevant from a data utility perspective in the long term.
This plan is structured according to the Horizon Europe template provided by the European Commission (Template – EU Grants: Data management plan (HE) v1.1 – 01.04.2022), adapted to align with CHIST-ERA requirements and recommendations, therefore contextualizing MultiGIS4Rivers overall approach to data management. In addition, a template has been designed following the same structure to gather specific issues for individual datasets which are adequately highlighted in the document as appropriate. The structure of this document is as follows:
  • Sections 4.1.1. and 4.1.2. provide for a data summary including an identification and contextualization of MultiGIS4Rivers research datasets and outputs under the scope of this plan.
  • A thorough assessment of FAIR principles is covered in section 4.1.3.
  • Section 4.1.4. encompasses the allocation of resources as well as responsibilities within data management.
  • Data security provisions as well as the management of ethical issues are covered in sections 4.1.5. and 4.1.6.
  • Section 4.1.7. relates to additional procedures for data management used within the Consortium.
This information of the last version available for the MultiGIS4Rivers datasets is managed at Consortium level as an excel book and will be kept updated (e.g., new data, data changes, changes in Consortium policies and/or other external factors).
The DMP has been conceived as a living document and will be revised throughout the project whenever significant changes arise. Accordingly, a new edition of the document will be issued as foreseen within the project proposal form: M9, M12, M18, M24, M30, and M36. Therefore, an efficient use of MultiGIS4Rivers resources within scientific-technical activities will be ensured. In addition, IPR and ethical issues management will be closely bonded to comply with MultiGIS4Rivers policies, contractual and regulatory obligations. 
In conclusion, this document approaches research data from a lifecycle perspective by means of identifying standardized curation and preservation measures to be taken to ensure an impact beyond the Consortium and the project duration. This is the first issue of the document (M3). As such, an effort has been performed to identify and contextualize MultiGIS4Rivers research datasets, although no homogeneous detailed information in the management of the data can be provided at this early stage.
plan.title: Data Management Plan for MultiGIS4Rivers Project

systemField.label: Description
systemField.description: Briefly describe the context and purpose of the DMP

systemField.label: Language
systemField.description: Select the language of your DMP

referenceField.label: Researchers
referenceField.description: Add here the names of people that have produced, processed, analysed the data described in the DMP.

referenceField.label: Organizations
referenceField.description: Add here the names of the organizations contributing to the creation and revision of the DMPs

referenceField.label: Funding organizations
referenceField.description: Select a funder of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Grants
referenceField.description: Find the grant of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Project
referenceField.description: Projects in Argos are perceived as distinct activities falling under a grant or common activities under different grants in collaborative schemas, eg open call for contributions. Please complete it for the grant associated to your organization if your project falls under this category. In all other cases, please leave blank and it will be autocompleted.

systemField.label: Access Rights
systemField.description: Choose how the DMP is displayed after is published on Zenodo. By choosing Open Access, the DMP will be open on Zenodo after the Publication Date. By choosing Restricted Access, the DMP will be restricted after the publication is made.

referenceField.label: License
referenceField.description: Assign a license to your DMP by selecting the most appropriate from the list.

reference.label: Finnish Geospatial Research Institute (FGI) in the National Land Survey of Finland
Haute École d'Ingénierie et de Gestion du Canton de Vaud HES-SO
CHIST-ERA-23; Proyectos de Colaboración Internacional PCI2025-1

description.description: This is an overview of the management of prospective MultiGIS4Rivers datasets, oriented to facilitate the lifecycle of research data. In addition, it aims at analyzing the alignment to FAIR principles striving for openness with the scope of maximizing data utility.
description.label: MultiGIS4Rivers Datasets Management Overview

descriptionTemplate.label: Horizon Europe
descriptionTemplate.description: This is the Argos instance of the Horizon Europe template.

selectData.label: Select from the list of options

fieldSet.title: What kind of research output are you describing?
fieldSet.description:
Research data is information (particularly facts or numbers) collected to be examined and considered, and to serve as a basis for reasoning, discussion or calculation. Open access to research data - the right to access and reuse digital research data under the terms and conditions set out in the Grant Agreement.

Other research outputs refer to outputs that are produced or reused during the research and data management lifecycle. They might be objects, instruments and materials in digital form, such as software, workflows, protocols, models, etc. Equally, in their analog form they might include new materials, antibodies, reagents, samples, etc.

fieldSet.title: Is it physical or digital?

labelData.label: Please specify your answer and state the reasons if re-use of any existing data has been considered but discarded.

fieldSet.title: Are you generating or re-using it?
fieldSet.description:
Apart from data produced within your research, you might want to repurpose data that have been produced and shared by others, in different research context.

If you are planning to use other researchers’ data in your research there are a couple of things that you need to take into consideration, such as copyright of datasets, permissions provided through licenses, ethical aspects for reuse, etc. If you are unsure of the license of a dataset that you are re-using, you may use the License Clearance Tool.

Learn more: https://www.openaire.eu/can-i-reuse-someone-else-research-data.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What is the type of the described dataset?
fieldSet.description:
The main distinction between data types is between primary data and secondary data. Data collection may contain both primary and secondary data depending on the source where they have been derived from. 
Primary data is data that have been collected for the first time and have not undergone through data processing and/or analysis, yet. 
Secondary data is data that have been cleaned up, analysed and shared by others (published or unpublished) and they are those that are being typically reused. 
In addition, types of data unveil how they have been collected so they also depend on the process that led to their generation.
For more information, have a look at: https://data.library.virginia.edu/data-management/plan/format-types/

labelData.label: Please specify format

fieldSet.title: What is its format?
fieldSet.description:
Different types of data are acquired, processed and stored (preserved and/or archived) in different ways and can be discipline specific. Many proprietary file formats are “containers” for standard file formats. By packaging them into these containers, a software and/or hardware developer can provide additional functionality, usually by streamlining a process, to analyse data acquired on their platform. However, this has the negative consequence of making these data less interoperable.

You may find some best practices for file formats: https://library.stanford.edu/research/data-management-services/data-best-practices/best-practices-file-formats
For more information about preserving data in appropriate formats: https://www.openaire.eu/data-formats-preservation-guide

labelData.label: Provide the byte size of the dataset.

fieldSet.title: What is its expected size?
fieldSet.description: Give measurable examples per type of output based on common practices in the field. These might be relevant to the volumes of data and how many bytes of storage they occupy, numbers of objects, files, rows, and columns.

selectData.label: Reason of new described dataset collection / generation

labelData.label: Please specify your answer by providing more details on your selection

fieldSet.title: Why are you collecting/generating or re-using it?
fieldSet.description: Data collection is usually at the beginning stages of research data management lifecycles to set the background of what is needed (data generation), what is already there (data reuse) and how to best use it to fulfill the project’s objectives (why). Here you may add information about the scope and objectives of your data collection process.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may find a comparison of different data management lifecycles here: Plale, Beth & Kouper, Inna. (2017). The Centrality of Data: Data Lifecycle and Data Pipelines. 10.1016/B978-0-12-809715-1.00004-3.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What is its origin / provenance?

labelData.label: Please specify your answer by providing more context and clear examples

fieldSet.title: To whom might it be useful ('data utility')?

section.description: Beneficiaries should consider which of the questions pertaining to FAIR data as defined in the HE DMP Template, can apply to the management of other research outputs, and should strive to provide sufficient detail on how their research outputs will be managed and shared, or made available for re-use, in line with the FAIR principles.
section.title: Brief description of the described research output

referenceTypeData.label: Select the journal where the publication can be found
Select the literature repository or the pre-print server where the publication can be found

fieldSet.title: Does the described output support any scientific publication?
fieldSet.description: Create the links between scientific publications and research data.

labelData.label: Provide URL with data access / availability statement

fieldSet.title: Is there a data availability statement provided along with the publication?

section.description:
Please complete this section for datasets that are important to validate scientific results described in publications.

Note: The European Commission offers Horizon Europe beneficiaries Open Research Europe (ORE), an open access publishing platform with no publishing fees. ORE is offered as an additional publishing option to Horizon Europe beneficiaries. When ORE is the selected publishing venue, all requirements for open access to scientific publications are fulfilled, as ORE deposits publications in the all-purpose repository Zenodo.
section.title: Publications

referenceTypeData.label: Select the data repository where the dataset can be found

fieldSet.title: Does the described output use or support any published dataset?

labelData.label: Add URL where the software can be found

fieldSet.title: Does the described output use or support any software?

section.description:
In general terms, your research data should be 'FAIR', that is findable, accessible, interoperable and re-usable. These principles precede implementation choices and do not necessarily suggest any specific technology, standard, or implementation-solution. Achieving FAIR data relies equally on the choices you make throughout a research process, but also on providers whose services enable FAIRness of data to be met. 

Learn more about the FAIR principles: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4792175/ 

Learn how you can comply with FAIR principles: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-make-your-data-fair
section.title: Making data and other outputs findable, including provisions for metadata

selectData.label: Please select the used Data Identifier System
Please select the used Researchers' Identifier System
Please select the used Organizations' Identifier System
Please select the used Projects' Identifier System

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: What type(s) of persistent identifier(s) are used for the described dataset / output?
fieldSet.description: Persistent and/or Permanent Identifiers (PIDs) uniquely identify objects, people, organisations and activities and can ensure that the scientific output is accessible even when the URL of the website has changed. PIDs can be assigned to research outputs including publications, data and software/code. PIDs can also be assigned to researchers, samples, organisations and projects. A PID may be connected to a metadata record describing an item rather than the item itself. PIDs are usually provided by data repositories and other deposit platforms. Re3data includes tags to show which platforms that it indexes assign PIDs to their content.

uploadData.label: If desired, upload an image of the used schema

labelData.label: Provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: Will you provide metadata for the described dataset / output?
fieldSet.description: Metadata is data about data and is an essential set of information describing scientific outputs, in the form of either physical or digital objects, in a machine-readable format. According to the expected use, metadata can be given different attributes. Most common type which enables discovery and identification are descriptive metadata. Descriptive metadata contain information about key aspects needed to search for and successfully find a given scientific output, e.g. by its title, author/creator, abstract, keywords. Moreover, metadata may be used for describing a service or a scientific instrument.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What type(s) of metadata?
fieldSet.description: Please outline what type of metadata will be created and how.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Do the metadata use standardised vocabularies?
fieldSet.description: Standardised vocabularies enable greater interoperability across systems. There are generic and discipline specific metadata standards which have been compiled so as to contain vital information that enable exchange while taking into consideration specificities of work and demands in different disciplines, even in different areas of work within a discipline.

labelData.label: URL where the vocabularies of the data can be found
Briefly describe selected vocabularies application for your needs

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Description of used vocabularies
Are the metadata searchable?
fieldSet.description: Searchable metadata are metadata indexed by search engines and are identifiable by web crawlers.

selectData.label: Metadata are indexed in/ by a

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: How are searchable metadata provided?

labelData.label: Please provide more detail for your answer

fieldSet.title: Are keywords provided in the metadata?
fieldSet.description: Keywords and tags help to optimize the possibility for discovery and then potential re-use of data.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: Are metadata harvestable?
fieldSet.description: Usually, this is facilitated by services that apply standard protocols for information exchange. For example, in repositories, metadata exchange is supported by the OAI-PMH protocol.

section.description: Data are findable when described with metadata and vocabularies in a standardized way, assigned Persistent Identifiers (PIDs) and are registered or indexed in a searchable resource.
section.title: Making data findable, including provisions for metadata

section.description: Not all data can be made publicly open, hence data can be FAIR but not open, or open but not FAIR or both FAIR and open. Data are accessible when uploaded in a data repository and retrieved by their PIDs. When data can not be shared openly, metadata should be provided (even when the data are no longer available). In the case of sensitive or personal data, anonymization or pseudonymization and specific access rights can be applied. Where accessing data requires the use of complementary methods or tools, such procedures should be documented.
section.title: Making data and other outputs openly accessible

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert the data repository
Add URL of the system hosting a distribution of a dataset.
Add host description

fieldSet.title: In which repository will the dataset / output be deposited?
fieldSet.description:
A data repository, otherwise known as a data archive, is an online collection of datasets that are described and classified in a standard way that makes data discovery and retrieval easier to be performed by both humans and machines. 

For more information on the processes for datasets, you can have a look at https://www.openaire.eu/rdm-handbook

selectData.label: What processes does it make use of?

options.label: Has an open access content policy
options.value: Has an open access content policy

options.label: Provides Open Access content (free at the point of use)
options.value: Provides Open Access content (free at the point of use)

options.label: Supports authentication and authorization of users
options.value: Supports authentication and authorization of users

options.label: Has data security mechanisms in place
options.value: Has data security mechanisms in place

fieldSet.title: Is the selected repository a trusted source?
fieldSet.description:
Trusted repositories assume a central role in the Horizon Europe for the deposition of and access to publications and research data. They are: 

  • Certified repositories (e.g. CoreTrustSeal, nestor Seal DIN31644, ISO16363) or disciplinary and domain repositories commonly used and endorsed by the research communities. Such repositories should be recognised internationally.
  • General-purpose repositories or institutional repositories that present the essential characteristics of trusted repositories, i.e.: 
    • display specific characteristics of organisational, technical and procedural quality such as services, mechanisms and/or provisions that are intended to secure the integrity and authenticity of their contents, thus facilitating their use and re-use in the short- and long-term. Trusted repositories have specific provisions in place and offer explicit information online about their policies, which define their services (e.g. acquisition, access, security of content, long-term sustainability of service including funding etc.).
    • provide broad, equitable and ideally open access to content free at the point of use, as appropriate, and respect applicable legal and ethical limitations. They assign persistent unique identifiers to contents (e.g. DOIs, handles, etc.), such that the contents (publications, data and other research outputs) are unequivocally referenced and thus citeable. They ensure that contents are accompanied by metadata sufficiently detailed and of sufficiently high quality to enable discovery, reuse and citation and contain information about provenance and licensing; metadata are machine- actionable and standardized (e.g. Dublin Core, Data Cite etc.) preferably using common non-proprietary formats and following the standards of the respective community the repository serves, where applicable.
    • facilitate mid- and long-term preservation of the deposited material. They have mechanisms or provisions for expert curation and quality assurance for the accuracy and integrity of datasets and metadata, as well as procedures to liaise with depositors where issues are detected. They meet generally accepted international and national criteria for security to prevent unauthorized access and release of content and have different levels of security depending on the sensitivity of the data being deposited to maintain privacy and confidentiality.

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert the name of certification(s)

fieldSet.title: Add certification(s)
fieldSet.description: Repository certified to a standard.

fieldSet.title: Add appropriate arrangements made with the repository(ies) where the described dataset will be deposited
Does the repository(ies) assign datasets / outputs with persistent identifiers?
fieldSet.description: Persistent identifiers (PIDs) are key in ensuring the findability of research outputs, including data. They are globally unique and long-lasting references to digital objects (such as data, publications and other research outputs) or non-digital objects such as researchers, research institutions, grants, etc. Frequently used persistent identifiers include digital object identifiers (DOIs), Handles, and others. For further reading on PID types, please refer to https://www.dpconline.org/handbook/technical-solutions-andtools/persistent-identifiers.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Does the repository(ies) resolve the identifiers to a digital object?
Does the repository support versioning?

labelData.label: Provide your answer

fieldSet.title: What is the described dataset / output title?

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: How is the dataset / output shared?
fieldSet.description: Indicates access mode for data. If certain datasets cannot be shared (or need to be shared under restricted access conditions), explain why, clearly separating legal and contractual reasons from intentional restrictions. Note that in multi-beneficiary projects it is also possible for specific beneficiaries to keep their data closed if opening their data goes against their legitimate interests or other constraints as per the Grant Agreement.

fieldSet.title: What is the reason of limiting access to the dataset / output?
fieldSet.description:
Explain why the described dataset or parts of its distribution cannot be shared (or need to be shared under restricted access conditions),clearly separating legal and contractual reasons from intentional restrictions. Note that in multi-beneficiary projects it is also possible for specific beneficiaries to keep their data closed if opening their data goes against their legitimate interests or other constraints as per the Grant Agreement.

Embargo periods are tied to sharing and publishing scientific outputs. An embargo might be applied to give time to publish or seek protection of the intellectual property (e.g. patents). If an embargo is applied to give time to publish or seek protection of the intellectual property (e.g. patents), specify why and how long this will apply, bearing in mind that research data should be made available as soon as possible.

labelData.label: Due date of effect of the embargo

fieldSet.title: If an embargo applies, please specify when the dataset / output will be made available.
fieldSet.description: If an embargo is applied to give time to publish or seek protection of the intellectual property (e.g. patents), specify why and how long this will apply, bearing in mind that research data should be made available as soon as possible.

referenceTypeData.label: Select EOSC service needed to access the dataset

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add tools names

fieldSet.title: Are there any methods or tools required to access the dataset / output?
fieldSet.description: Describe processes needed to query and access data. These might be relevant to SPARQL access points, visualizer software, download, etc. Data access controls, such as passwords or firewalls, should be used to limit access to confidential data and protect them from unauthorised changes.

labelData.label: Briefly explain the method(s) need to be applied for data access
URL where the method can be found

fieldSet.title: Please provide information about the method(s) needed to access the dataset / output.

labelData.label: Briefly describe the tool needed to be used for data access
Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add used tool

fieldSet.title: Please provide information about the tools needed to access the dataset / output.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Is the described dataset / output supported by a data access committee?
fieldSet.description: Explain if there is a need for a dedicated data access committee to evaluate/approve access requests to personal/sensitive data, etc.

labelData.label: Please provide details on your strategy
Please specify how the identity of the person accessing the data will be ascertained

fieldSet.title: Please specify how the dataset / output will be accessed during and after the project ends

labelData.label: Please specify the time period

fieldSet.title: Please specify how long after the project has ended the dataset / output will be made accessible for
fieldSet.description: Please check with funders' or institutions's policies which might apply an embargo on data access.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Will you provide metadata even if the described dataset / output can not be openly shared?

labelData.label: Creative Commons Zero (CC0)
Other
Please specify the use of other license

fieldSet.title: Under which license will metadata be provided?
fieldSet.description: According to the Grant Agreement, metadata are made openly available and licensed under a public domain dedication CC0.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Do metadata provide information about how to access the described dataset / output?

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Will metadata remain available after the dataset / output is no longer available?

section.description: To enhance the findability of research outputs, and their potential reuse, standardised metadata frameworks are essential, ensuring that data and other research outputs are accompanied by rich metadata that provides them with context.
section.title: Metadata

labelData.label: Add vocabulary

fieldSet.title: Does your (meta)data use a controlled vocabulary?
fieldSet.description: Controlled vocabularies provide standard terminology as opposed to keywords or tags used to classify information. Examples: taxonomies, ontologies, thesauri.

labelData.label: Please specify

uploadData.label: If desired, upload an image of the schema created

fieldSet.title: If you created the vocabulary, where can it be found?
fieldSet.description: Consider openly publishing the generated ontologies or vocabularies to allow reusing, refining or extending them.

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add used metadata standard(s)

fieldSet.title: Have you applied a standard schema for your (meta)data?
fieldSet.description: You may browse the Metadata Standards Catalog here: http://rd-alliance.github.io/metadata-directory/

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Will you provide a mapping to more commonly used ontologies?

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What is the methodology followed?

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: What community-endorsed interoperability best practices are followed?

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Does the described dataset / output provide qualified references with other outputs?
fieldSet.description: A qualified reference is a cross-reference that explains its intent. For example, X is regulator of Y is a much more qualified reference than X is associated with Y, or X see also Y. The goal therefore is to create as many meaningful links as possible between (meta)data resources to enrich the contextual knowledge about the data. (Source: https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/i3-metadata-include-qualified-references-metadata/)

section.description: Data are interoperable, meaning they can be easily understood and shared with other platforms and systems, when they are created using standard vocabularies and include references to other data and metadata.
section.title: Making data and other outputs interoperable

labelData.label: If an embargo applies, please specify when the dataset will be made openly available
Provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What internationally recognised licence will you use for your dataset / output?
fieldSet.description:
There are a number of licenses that can be assigned to your research outputs. There are different licenses for software and for publications and data. For the latter, a common practice is the use of Creative Commons licenses which are machine readable.

For more information on the licences required for data under Horizon Europe, please refer to the AGA (article 17).

You may learn more about how to choose a license here: https://www.openaire.eu/how-do-i-license-my-research-data
You may use the License Clearance Tool (LCT) to check compliance of licenses for derivative works: https://lct.ni4os.eu/

labelData.label: Please specify your answer

fieldSet.title: What reusability and / or reproducibility methods are followed?
fieldSet.description: Provide supplementary material that validate data analysis and facilitate data re-use.

fieldSet.title: Will you provide the described dataset / output in the public domain?

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: Do you intend to ensure (re)use by third parties after your project finishes?
fieldSet.description: Despite where data are stored for everyday activities during the project lifetime, there are certain options to choose from data storage that ensures reuse in the log run. Most popular are data archives, otherwise found as repositories. Depending of data volume, archiving might be subject to small fees.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: Is provenance well documented?
fieldSet.description: Data provenance, or data lineage, is a type of metadata that tracks digital object's history in time and its relations with other entities. You may also check: https://casrai.org/term/provenance-metadata/

options.label: Set up of scientific and technical committee
options.value: Set up of scientific and technical committee

options.label: Use of tools for automatic checks
options.value: Use of tools for automatic checks

options.label: Consistency verified with data models and standards
options.value: Consistency verified with data models and standards

labelData.label: Provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: What documented procedures for quality assurance do you have in place?
fieldSet.description:
Quality assurance procedures are closely related to RDM and are meant to validate that data are clean (no duplicates or inconsistencies), error-free, well structured and represented in analysis. Quality assurance checks can be facilitated by the use of tools or scripts.

To familiarise yourself with the process, you may find a checklist here: https://www.eur.nl/sites/corporate/files/2017-11/Checklist_QA_QC_RDM_0_2_MD_20150129.pdf

section.description: Data can be reused when the conditions about how others can make use of the data are well-described following community-standards and are communicated as specified by the owners. Such information can be found in licenses attributed to data and in references about the data provenance.
section.title: Increasing data and other outputs reuse

labelData.label: Add value
Add name other RDM activity associated with the cost
Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What will be the cost of making the described output FAIR?
fieldSet.description: To understand what costs are allocated across the steps of a RDM lifecycle, how to estimate them and how to prevent them, please check: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-comply-to-h2020-mandates-rdm-costs

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: How will this cost be covered?
fieldSet.description: Note that costs related to research data/output management are eligible as part of the Horizon Europe grant (if compliant with the Grant Agreement conditions).

referenceTypeData.label: Search by using ORCIDs of people responsible for data management

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add name
Please specify the role

fieldSet.title: Identify the people who will be responsible and their role(s) in the management of the described output
fieldSet.description:
Provide names and responsibilities of researchers’ or data managers’ data management and
stewardship activities that are performed throughout the project for the described output. Follow the research data
management and software management lifecycles to guide your steps in data capture, software development,, metadata production, data quality,
storage and backup, data and software archiving and sharing.

section.description: Data management can be costly, especially when its planning hasn't been sufficient from the very beginning of the research process. Costing of data management includes for example potential use of proprietary services and tools or extra effort needed to perform specific tasks or even to develop tools from scratch.
section.title: Allocation of resources

labelData.label: Please specify your selection

fieldSet.title: What security measures are followed?
fieldSet.description: Describe what provisions are in place for data security.

selectData.label: Select from the list of answers

labelData.label: Please specify your answer

fieldSet.title: What conditions do the security measures meet?
fieldSet.description: Describe data provisions, including data recovery as well as secure storage/archiving and transfer of sensitive data.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: How will you preserve the described dataset / output in the long term?
fieldSet.description: Please describe curation and preservation policies followed for repository content.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answers
Indicate (URL) where a protocol from a meeting with an ethical committee can be found.

fieldSet.title: Are there any ethical or legal issues that can have an impact on sharing the described dataset / output?
Does the described dataset / output contain sensitive information?
Does the described dataset / output contain personal data?

visibilityRule.value: Data accompanied by informed consent statements

options.label: Privacy constraints and applicable ethical norms
options.value: Privacy constraints and applicable ethical norms

options.label: Data accompanied by informed consent statements
options.value: Data accompanied by informed consent statements

selectData.label: Will informed consent for data sharing and long term preservation be included in questionnaires dealing with personal data?

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: What are the methods used for processing and accessing sensitive/personal information?
Do you make use of other procedures for data management?
fieldSet.description: Please check: https://www.scienceeurope.org/our-resources/guidance-document-presenting-a-framework-for-discipline-specific-research-data-management, https://www.dtls.nl/fair-data/research-data-management/data-management-knowledge-tools/

labelData.label: Please provide links (URLs) and additional information on your answer

fieldSet.title: Documentation of other procedures

field.textValue:
As established in the CHIST-ERA 2023 call, each publication must acknowledge the CHIST-ERA support and indicate the Project Identifier, for instance: “This work was supported by the CHIST-ERA grant [insert the Project Identifier], by [insert the funding organisation and the grant number for that funding organisation], [insert the next funding organisation and the grant number for that funding organisation], etc.”.
Project partners must include at least the following funder references in the generated metadata for retrieval purposes (see https://www.chistera.eu/call-2023-announcement/):
  • This work was supported by the CHIST-ERA grant CHIST-ERA-23-MultiGIS-06.
  • The specifications included in the respective funding organizations of each member of the Consortium, according to their rules and guidelines.

Formats for observational/simulation data include but are not limited to .csv, .txt, .xls, .docx, .jpg, .mp4, .nc, .las, and others (TBD). Derived or compiled data formats include .py/.ipynb, .tif, .nc, and others (TBD).
Even if a dataset is not granted open access (free of charge), FAIR principles still are to be considered to improve data management and strive to maximize the impact of the MultiGIS4Rivers research.

Data security provisions will be in terms of storage and recovery. Particularities on sensitive data and long-term preservation provisions are also covered. If sensitive data is to be managed, it is recommended to assess the feasibility of anonymization, pseudonymization or encryption techniques, whilst still preserving the data utility. In addition, restricted licenses or closed access should be considered. Personal data generated through workshops, focus groups and interviews/surveys will be treated according to the EU’s GDPR. In addition, consent forms will be collected so that sensitive data (e.g. photos of people) can be published. When long-term preservation is envisaged, a trustworthy repository would be recommended, if possible, choosing a certified repository.

Any ethical issue that may arise within the MultiGIS4Rivers project, particularly regarding its impact in research and research data will be managed according to the established procedures and mechanisms of reporting. The project will refrain from systematically gathering personal information. However, certain data may contain sensitive elements, such as those pertinent to national security. In such cases, precautions will be taken when disclosing data, ensuring that datasets containing potentially harmful or privacy-compromising information remain closed. Facial recognition will be obscured if present in the data. Third parties will not be granted access to the original raw data or measurement documentation by default, citing reasons including the volume of data, security concerns, and privacy considerations. High-quality, meticulously documented curated data will be made accessible and disseminated. Plots will be established in areas either owned by institutes/universities of the Consortium or with full approval from the respective owners. To ensure that all research activities are conducted with the highest standards of integrity and responsibility, each partner within the MultiGIS4Rivers project has formally committed to adhering to their respective institutional codes of ethics and internationally recognized guidelines. Partner-specific ethical commitments are summarized below.
1. UVIGO
  • Adherence to the Code of Ethics of the University of Vigo, which promotes responsibility, exemplary conduct and transparency among all university members
  • Commitment to the values of openness in research and science
2. FGI
  • Commitment to the guidelines laid out by the Finnish Advisory Board on Research Integrity (TENK)
  • Compliance with All European Academics Code of Conduct
3. HEIG-VD
  • Commitment to its internal rules and guidelines promoting research integrity and sustainable development
  • Adherence to the Code of Conduct for Scientific Integrity of the Swiss Academies of Arts and Sciences
  • Compliance with All European Academics Code of Conduct
4. AMU
  • Commitment to the Adam Mickiewicz University guidelines regarding ethical issues in a research project
  • Compliance with All European Academics Code of Conduct
5. BUT
  • Commitment to the BUT Code of Ethics regarding ethical challenges of research projects
  • Compliance with All European Academics Code of Conduct
6. UNICAMP
  • Commitment to the institutional policy of good practices and integrity in Research at Unicamp
  • Adherence to national guidelines and standards promoted by the Brazilian National Council for Scientific and Technological Development (CNPq)
The ethical evaluation is undertaken in all research. All people working in the project will be treated under similar rules that follow the regulations of the respective partner institutions. 
The MultiGIS4Rivers project supports many of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) towards 2030, mostly the work towards climate actions (13), life on land (15), industry innovation and infrastructure (9), clean water and sanitation (6), quality education (4), and decent work and economic growth (8), but links to other SDG’s can be seen as well.

The utility of each prospective MultiGIS4Rivers dataset is detailed in section 4.1.1.1.4.
The assessment of data utility in the long term (even after the project termination) encompasses both Consortium partners’ interests as well as MultiGIS4Rivers stakeholders’ community. Having in mind potential data and knowledge integration and reuse is a key conduit leading to knowledge discovery and innovation.
Apart from affecting the initial plan for data management, data utility may play a role in the decision-making regarding:  
  • Long-term preservation and curation provisions
  • Form of data to be shared/made available (e.g., raw data, processed and/or analyzed)
  • Data usability by third parties (e.g., data licensing and/or restrictions).
No relevant information is yet available at the reporting date concerning specific search keywords for the MultiGIS4Rivers datasets.

MultiGIS4Rivers datasets will be made accessible for at least three to five years beyond the project’s lifetime (and preferably 10 years or even longer), according to specific needs and/or disciplinary deposition practices.
The origin of each prospective MultiGIS4Rivers dataset is detailed in section 4.1.1.1.4.
GBs/TBs (TBD)

Yes. Zenodo assigns persistent identifiers (Digital Object Identifiers, DOIs) to all deposited records, and each DOI resolves to a stable landing page that provides access to the digital object, its metadata, versioning information, and associated files. The DOI is registered through DataCite, ensuring long-term persistence and resolvability. Refer to Zenodo's official principles for additional information: https://about.zenodo.org/principles/.
No relevant information is yet available at the reporting date for the methods needed to access MultiGIS4Rivers datasets.

Prospective MultiGIS4Rivers datasets are described below in terms of dataset name, work package/task, origin (data type), description and purpose as well as utility in relation to the objectives of the project.
  1. Field data from test sites  - Observational (WP3/T3.1, WP2/T2.1, WP2/T2.2, WP2/T2.3): In situ observations on riparian vegetation, hydrological, atmospheric, and other environmental variables collected from test sites via novel monitoring systems (e.g., IoT sensors, citizen science, UAV, remote communications) for the purpose of providing AI-ready models for impact analysis. Data will be used to provide ground truth for model training and validation.
  2. Remote sensing and curated datasets - Observational (WP3/T3.2): Multi-source and multi-scale remote sensing data from different satellite missions (e.g., Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2), alongside curated datasets (e.g., ERA5) compiled with in-situ measurements for integration in data fusion models. Data will be used as input for data fusion and environmental modeling.
  3. Synthetic climate and environmental dataset - Simulation (WP3/T3.3): Synthetic dataset generated from the spatio-temporal interpolation of climate and environmental data using deep generative AI methods for integration in data fusion models. Data could be useful for providing gap-filled data for model training.
  4. Multi-source multi-scale data (output from fusion models) - Derived or compiled (WP3/T3.2): Data generated using foundation models (e.g., ClimaX, Pengu-Weather, FourCastNet, and W-MAE) for accurately forecasting future meteorological conditions and modelling the spatiotemporal characteristics of freshwater ecosystems. Data will be used as harmonized analysis-ready inputs for computational and predictive models.
  5. Socio-economic datasets - Observational (WP4/T4.1): Multi-source data providing socio-economic proxies collected for the purpose of integrating socio-economic factors within analysis of land use and habitat changes. Data could be useful for incorporating governance, land use conflicts, and risk perception into environmental modelling.
  6. Computational methods for land use and habitat changes - Derived or compiled (WP4/T4.1): Computational outputs (land use metrics, detailed riparian vegetation classes), and reports/maps to track land use and habitat changes, and provide year-on-year trends for further analysis (e.g., change predictions, impact). Data could be useful for conservation planning and development of effective land management strategies through the monitoring of land use and habitat dynamics.
  7. Rainfall datasets - Observational (WP4/T4.2): Rainfall observation data collected via traditional methods (e.g., radar images, rain gauges) and opportunistic sensing (e.g., personal weather stations., rainfall derived microwave links, satellite networks) to develop computational methods for accurate rainfall observations as input for evaluating climate change impacts on water quality and quantity. Data will be used to calibrate and validate computational methods for rainfall observations.
  8. Combined computational methods for rainfall observation - Derived or compiled (WP4/T4.2): Algorithmic outputs that combine traditional observations with opportunistic sensing to produce precise rainfall data for the purpose of enhancing the assessment of climate change effects on water resources. Data could be useful for improving the accuracy and reliability of hydrological models.
  9. Earth Observation (EO) and LiDAR data - Observational (WP4/T4.3): High spatio-temporal resolution EO data from satellite missions (Copernicus Sentinel-1, Sentinel-2), and multi-temporal multi-spectral LiDAR data assimilated into science-based expert models and process-based predisposition models for the purpose of developing computational methods that evaluate he relationship between impacts of main drivers on environmental hazards (e.g., flooding, forest fires, biodiversity loss risk). Data will be used to provide structural and spectral features for hazard and risk modeling.
  10. Computational methods for main drivers of flooding, forest fires, biodiversity loss - Derived or compiled (WP4/T4.3): Algorithmic outputs and reports/map describing the main drivers of floods, forest fires, and biodiversity loss for developing an innovative prediction framework to assess and mitigate environmental threats. Data could be useful for risk assessment, environmental management, and designing prevention strategies against environmental threats.
  11. Computational algorithms for tree species - Derived or compiled (WP5/T5.1): Algorithmic outputs obtained from Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) models for assessment of riparian vegetation in terms of spectral-spatial distribution and temporal evolution. Data could be useful for biodiversity monitoring, forestry management, and classification of riparian vegetation.
  12. Predictive models of changes in ecological corridors - Derived or compiled (WP5/T5.2): Models developed in Python to assess the current state and predict future changes in ecological corridors of freshwater ecosystems. Data could be useful for ecological connectivity studies, conservation planning, and ecological modeling.
  13. BioClim climatic data - Observational/Simulation (WP5/T5.3): Climatic data retrieved from the BioClim website for the training of empirical models during the implementation and evaluation of different climate and action scenarios. Data will be used for model training and validation in scenario analysis.
  14. Computational algorithms for the evaluation of climate and action scenarios - Derived or compiled (WP5/T5.3): Algorithmic outputs from the best fitting empirical models applied to climate and land use change scenarios. Data could be useful for evaluating management options and supporting policy development under different future scenarios.
  15. Risk assessment before and after nature-based solutions (NbS) - Derived or compiled (WP5/T5.4): Outputs from the comparison of predictive model results for risk management assessment before and after implementation of NbS. Data could be useful for informing stakeholders and decision makers on the effectiveness of NbS.

textListValues.textListValue: Has an open access content policy
Provides Open Access content (free at the point of use)
Supports authentication and authorization of users
Has data security mechanisms in place

field.textValue:
If scientific peer-reviewed publications are produced then they must be published under open licenses (such as Creative Commons), providing specific minimum sets of rights of reuse. A CC BY license must be used for articles and book chapters in edited books, while CC BY, CC BY-NC, CC BY-ND, CC BY-NC-ND or equivalent are allowed for long-text formats.
Research data should be deposited in a trusted repository as soon as possible after data production and at the latest by the end of the project and must be licensed accordingly following the principle “as open as possible, as closed as necessary”. It is also recommended to license other research outputs (software, workflows, etc.) under appropriate licenses.
Some references for selecting an adequate license are:
  • https://ufal.github.io/public-license-selector/
  • https://b2share.eudat.eu/ (This tool includes a built-in license wizard that facilitates the selection of an adequate license for research data)
  • It is recommended to use Creative Commons Attributions as applicable (CC-BY-SA-NC-ND acronyms ShareAlike, NonCommercial, NoDerivs).
Regarding software, it should be noted that apart from CC0 (i.e. public domain dedication), CC licenses are not appropriate (although they can be used for software documentation). Instead, the use of open-source licenses appropriate for software is strongly recommended.
Metadata of deposited publications and datasets must be open under a Creative Common Public Domain Dedication (CC0) or equivalent.
MultiGIS4Rivers Datasets
No relevant information is yet available at the reporting date concerning additional metadata types for the MultiGIS4Rivers datasets.
Restrictions may apply, refer to sections 4.1.3.2.2.9. and 4.1.3.4.1. for more information.
No relevant information is yet available at the reporting date regarding the controlled vocabulary used in metadata for MultiGIS4Rivers datasets.

MultiGIS4Rivers datasets’ template has been designed in line with DataCite Metadata Schema Documentation properties and standard values (refer to http://schema.datacite.org/ for up-to-date information). No relevant information is yet available at the reporting date concerning specific metadata provisions (information available, standard and identified) or search keywords for the MultiGIS4Rivers datasets.

Research data within CHEST-ERA funding is expected to adhere to the approach “as open as possible, as closed as necessary”. Data accessibility is expected to be improved if relevant information and/or access is provided regarding:
  • Methods or software tools needed for data access
  • Software documentation and other information needed.
Refer to section 4.1.3.2.2.9. for more information in this topic.
No relevant information is yet available at the reporting date for the tools needed to access MultiGIS4Rivers datasets.
TBD

The allocation of resources for MultiGIS4Rivers data management will focus on the costs to make the research data FAIR (e.g., covering provisions such as: metadata, identifier, repository, secure storage, anonymization, and other needed curation/preservation actions). No relevant information is available at the reporting date for detailed description of estimated data management costs.
Direct cost

For the identification of a suitable repository for the research dataset, reference is made to OpenDOAR (https://v2.sherpa.ac.uk/opendoar/), the global registry of research data repositories (https://www.re3data.org/), and the Collector of Open Science for Spain (https://recolecta.fecyt.es/). As such:
  • Preference should be given to certified repositories which support open access
  • Disciplinary repositories would be preferred
  • Alternatively, the institutional repository could be used (if long term availability is granted).
Science Europe provides detailed guidelines for the selection of appropriate and trustworthy repositories, focusing on criteria related to the provision of Persistent and Unique Identifiers (PIDs), metadata, data access and usage licenses, as well as preservation (see Science Europe. (2021). Practical Guide to the International Alignment of Research Data Management - Extended Edition. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.4915862).
Software and models are foreseen to be shared via GitHub. Datasets that are foreseen to be open will be shared and preserved through trusted repositories. If there are no relevant standards in the discipline and disciplinary/institutional repositories are not to be used, depositing datasets in Zenodo (https://zenodo.org/) as a default option guarantees compatibility with OpenAIRE. As a free catch-all repository for EC funded research:
  • Offers the possibility to house closed and restricted content. Protected links can be shared, and content can be embargoed
  • Generates metadata compliant to OpenAIRE (exportable in several standard formats), assigns a DOI for each upload and enables versioning features
  • Allows for GitHub integration
  • At the moment, Zenodo does not have any certification yet.

Access to MultiGIS4Rivers research data may be assessed as:
  • Open
  • Embargoed
  • Restricted
  • Closed
Restrictions may be based in legal (i.e., ethics) or contractual (i.e., exploitation based) issues. Conditions for access may apply (i.e., researchers) and/or may be subject to a specific timing. Closed data (e.g. those subject to prior intellectual property rights) will be stored and shared amongst authorised partners and stakeholder members through cloud storage and file-sharing providers (e.g. Microsoft Teams), ensuring first that they comply with the relevant requirements of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR; 2016/679 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27 April 2016) and applicable laws and regulations. In case a dataset cannot be shared as is, an assessment should be performed to consider the possibility to share the research data in any other maturity state (i.e., raw data). The overall strategy adopted by MultiGIS4Rivers for the open access to publications, research data and software is detailed below.
1. Scientific publications
Scientific publications from the MultiGIS4Rivers project include peer-reviewed journal publications, peer-reviewed conference papers, books and book chapters.
All project publications must be available in Open Access. This can be achieved via the Green, Gold or Diamond Road. Neither embargos nor hybrid journals are allowed.
  • Green road: publications are deposited in an open archive without any embargo.
  • Golden road: research is published in an Open Access to publications venue .
  • Diamond road : corresponding venues are non-profit, managed by disciplinary communities and enable Open Access publication without charges.
The full text of the publication (version Author Accepted Manuscript - AAM or publisher version - VoR) will be deposited on an open repository, at the time of publication at the latest, with a reference to the CHIST-ERA support and by retaining sufficient intellectual property rights through the Rights Retention Strategy (see https://www.coalition-s.org/rights-retention-strategy/). Project partners are encouraged to systematically publish preprints. To enhance transparency and reproducibility, the (pre-) registration of research outputs (pre-registered and registered reports) is recommended. The systematic publication of negative results is also strongly recommended, including all related research data and software, based on the same guidelines put forward in the present DMP. Possible publication costs are eligible as part of the grant, if they fulfil the general eligibility conditions specified by the respective funding organizations, according to their rules and guidelines.
2. Underlying research data and software (supporting open access publications)
If publication is chosen as a means of dissemination, then MultiGIS4Rivers project partners must aim at making available the underlying data (i.e., research data needed to validate the results presented in the publication) at latest with the publication of the corresponding research outputs and appropriately linked to the latter, with a reference to the CHIST-ERA grant. All software published by the project must be available under an open-source license.
It is relevant to mention that:
  • Open research data repositories that enable FAIR data will be considered as main option
  • To facilitate contribution and reuse, software development will be hosted on a platform that allows collaborative development
  • Motivated (i.e. research bound by legal, ethical, copyright, confidentiality or other clauses) restricted access or use is feasible (in this case, the metadata must be deposited on a FAIR-enabling data repository)
  • Confidentiality to preserve legitimate interests (especially for MultiGIS4Rivers business partners and exploitation intentions) may condition a request for preventing open access to the data
  • Security issues, rules on protecting personal data and other legitimate reasons may also be analyzed.
In addition:
  • Authors are encouraged to retain their copyright and grant adequate licenses to publishers enabling third parties to access, mine, reproduce and disseminate (free of charge for any user) this research data (e.g., attaching a Creative Commons License to the data deposited)
  • Costs related to open access are eligible as part of the grant, if they fulfil the general eligibility conditions specified by the respective funding organizations, according to their rules and guidelines
3. Other research data
When producing new tools, reviews and grey literature (i.e. outputs), these will be made available as editorial open access. However, the binding compromises regarding granting open access to MultiGIS4Rivers research data (apart from underlying data as previously motivated) are not applicable. To the extent possible the opportunity to deposit MultiGIS4Rivers research datasets other than underlying data will be explored and highlighted throughout this document considering the guidelines established in this management plan.

MultiGIS4Rivers research may also benefit from data re-use. This data may or may not be considered background (for Consortium Agreement contractual purposes).
Background means any and all, data, information, know-how, IPRs that is/are: (i) owned or controlled by a Party prior to the Effective Date; or (ii) developed or acquired by a Party independently from the work in the Action even if in parallel with the performance of the Action, but solely to the extent that such data, information, know-how and/or IPRs are introduced into the Action by the owning Party.
Provisions set up in this document will be applicable to this data (either considered background or not) as appropriate within the scope of adequate data management. In addition, background data will be subject to Consortium Agreement provisions, in terms of access rights as well as dissemination protection/approval [as per Consortium Agreement section 9.1 and attachment 1 (background included)].
New
Refer to section 4.1.3.2.2.9. for more information regarding cases where alternative data access options (other than open access) may be considered.

It is recommended to arrange following provisions, as applicable:
  • Metadata (compatible with OpenAIRE standards built on the DataCite Metadata Schema)
  • Standard identification mechanism (DOI or equivalent)
  • Naming conventions and clear versioning approach (refer to https://semver.org/ for additional information).
In practice, these topics are to be considered in the identification of a suitable repository for the research dataset. Refer to section 4.1.3.2.1.4. for guidelines and recommendations in this topic.
No relevant information is yet available at the reporting date regarding data security provisions for the MultiGIS4Rivers datasets.

Costs related to open access to research data are eligible as part of the CHIST-ERA 2023 grant (if they fulfil the general eligibility conditions specified by the respective funding organizations, according to their rules and guidelines).
The purpose for collecting/generating each prospective MultiGIS4Rivers dataset in relation to the objectives of the project is detailed in section 4.1.1.1.4.

Responsible for data management at high level. Their roles include:
  • Maintenance, update and revision of Project Management Plan
  • Update and revision of dataset(s) templates
  • Guidance and advice on demand
  • Oversight of potential impact in exploitation/dissemination activities
Coordinator Support Team (UVIGO)

Responsible for data management at low level. Their roles include:
  • Design of the research data lifecycle (including decision-making in long term provisions)
  • Update and revision of dataset(s) contents
  • Adherence to commitments within the DPM
  • Raise awareness of potential impact in exploitation/dissemination activities
  • Contact person for the dataset
Project partner

Responsible for data management at intermediate level (joint datasets). Their roles include:
  • Overall strategy for joint datasets (more than one project partner involved)
  • Raise awareness of potential impact in exploitation/dissemination activities
Task/WP leader

To facilitate the planning and the proper implementation of the Open Science policy within the project, every Consortium must assign the role of Open Science Coordinator to one of its members, who will be the main contact point for CHIST-ERA concerning Open Science-related matters. 
For the MultiGIS4Rivers project, this role is assigned to the Project Coordinator (UVIGO).
The Open Science Coordinator will be responsible for:
  • Planning dissemination activities in connection with all scientific deliverables
  • Adapting the budget to cover for the Open Science costs
  • Submission and update of the DMP, following up with Open Science aspects during the lifetime of the project and coordinating these aspects with the elaboration of exploitation plans for project deliverables that require specific intellectual property strategies (e.g. patenting or commercialization)
  • Liaison with project members with respect to Open Access to publications and research data/software aspects
  • Analysis of Open Science practices in other projects.
Open Science Coordinator (UVIGO)
No relevant information is yet available at the reporting date regarding specific vocabularies used in the metadata to describe MultiGIS4Rivers datasets.

Apart from underlying data, it is recommended to assess datasets for long term provisions in terms of uniqueness, long-term value and/or reuse potential.
Project partners must aim to preserve research data for a substantial period – at least three to five years beyond the project’s lifetime, and preferably 10 years or even longer – according to specific needs and/or disciplinary deposition practices. The source code of the software must be stored together with all its development history in the Software Heritage archive in order to ensure long term availability of the project results. The source code must also be referenced with curated metadata following the DataCite Metadata Schema in an open repository based on the FAIR data principle. For preservation purposes, it is recommended to carefully select data formats. For curation purposes, it is recommended to assess services provided by the repository supplier. Support for data re-use after the end of project may also be considered.

MultiGIS4Rivers activities within WP6, specifically task 6.2, focus on the implementation of the data storage functionality within the modelling platform under development. This task will have a significant impact on data management and will therefore be considered in future updates of this document. A brief overview is presented in this section.
FGI leads task 6.2 of the MultiGIS4Rivers project, which will implement the data storage functionality for the modelling platform. The platform will store all the relevant datasets that are needed to provide the required functionality of the platform within the practical limitations. The datasets will be a combination of raw, processed, and post-processed data, as well as various predictions of the models created in the project. The goal is to provide different access methods to the data on the platform, including direct download and standard APIs, such as the OGC (Open Geospatial Consortium) API family.
The data will be primarily stored using open data formats when suitable formats exist. The metadata of the datasets can be used to search and filter the data on the platform. The lifetime of the platform will follow that of the project’s and does not consider the long-time preservation needs of the data generated within the project. It is meant to provide a common working platform for partners from different organizations.
(i) Data security
Data security will be addressed by two points of view: preserving the data and limiting access to the data.
a. Data preservation
  • The data will be stored using open formats that are able to preserve e.g. the original accuracy of the measurement data. The data may be compressed but the compression formats need to be lossless.
  • By default, the datasets on the platform are stored in read-only mode to prevent accidental modification or deletion of the data.
  • The backups of the stored data will be maintained by the platform and/or by the partner who provided the data.
b. Data access
  • Connection to the platform is always encrypted, for example using SSL-certificates for http connections.
  • Some datasets generated during the project can be public and made accessible e.g. using the platform. Access to other datasets can be more limited. The platform provides an option to limit access to the datasets on the per-user basis.
  • By default, sensitive or confidential material is not stored on the platform but transferred by other means if needed and stored in an encrypted form.
  • Access to the platform will be logged and followed to identify potential security issues.
(ii) Intellectual Property Rights Management
Data management will be aligned with MultiGIS4Rivers Consortium Agreement [section 9 (Access Rights) and attachment 1 (background included)].
Access Rights to background data, results, and software will be granted to Parties as needed for project work or exploitation under fair, non-exclusive, and confidential terms, including provisions for entering or leaving the Consortium and GDPR compliance.
Digital
Zenodo
Metadata will identify the required methods, software and documentation in case they are needed for MultiGIS4Rivers accessible datasets.
